speaker: interviewee
text: --9th between Walsh and-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the other one? Maple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you ready? Mike?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we gotta go back to Melbourne. Well, you can-- you can just start saying about your retirement. And then just say that you mentioned-- All right. Yes, all right. You can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I left [? Sig Isaacs ?] and I decided to go to [INAUDIBLE]. Of course, like every beginning, this was hard, too. But I got to know people. I used to buy merchandise from different people. And it was pretty good to us. We worked hard. But it was good to us. My wife worked for about 10 years in home sewing shop. And then after a while, I said, look, it's getting awfully hard for me. I did it all by myself. I think you should give up your job. And she came to work with me, which made it a hell of a lot easier. I mean, there's no comparison.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, she's a terrific sales lady. And it took off the responsibility of me watching, doing everything. How can [? I ?] do it by myself? When I think about it now, it's impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 20 years was enough. We did it for 20 years. And things became bad in business, competition, and everything. And I got older, and I just couldn't do it. So finally, we managed-- luckily, we managed to get out. I think it was March of 1990. We got out of it. And thinking about business, and I remember some, we were talking about Wolkowicz-- about the guy that beat me up in Birkenau when I-- when I reported them that they were stealing. Where I worked the last five years, when I managed that little store, it was near a corner. The door was locked. It wasn't open door. The customers couldn't come in and out. I had to ring the bell, and I opened up, because there was nobody in the store to sell. I was in the back in the factory. If somebody came in-- it's not the kind of thing that you come in every five minutes and buy something. The doorbell rings. I open the door. And I nearly died. Wolkowicz came in-- the guy that beat me up in Auschwitz. And I recognized him immediately. If this was where I worked, at that point, this had to be anywhere between '60 and '65. Let's say 1961 or '62, which was so many years after I'd seen him in Lodz, where the police let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came in. And I said to him, Wolkowicz? He said, yes. You know me? I said, yes. Don't you remember? And I told him the story. That guy went crazy. He didn't know what to do. He left the store, go downtown. Well, I couldn't sleep after that. I said, how can I let him live? How can I let him here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw where he walked. He had a little store right diagonally across the street, in the same corner. A repair shop-- he was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same evening, I walked over there. Apparently, he told his wife. And I told his wife, I said, what the hell do I do with him? Do I beat him up here, or do I call the police or something? I told my friends. I said, hey, listen. Let's finish him off here. I said, hey, we're not in Germany now. That's not Poland after the war. Even Poland after the war, I couldn't do it. This is Australia. How do I hit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she begged me not to touch him. The guy's got a heart condition. I said, I touch him, he might die. And I'll go-- and I'll go to jail for the rest of my life for killing somebody. That's all I need. But I didn't give him any peace. I walked past that store everyday and talked to his wife. And she begged me, please, don't tell the-- I think he had a boy. Don't tell the children. It would kill him. I said, it would be a real good idea. That's-- that's what I intend to do. But you know, after a while, I just let it slip. I let it go. I didn't report him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what could I do in Australia so many years later? I would have to have-- in Australia, they wouldn't even arrest him. It would be my word against his. He wouldn't come out and say, yes, I'm guilty. I did that. And witnesses-- forget it. At that time? So many years later. 20 years later-- 18 years later. So I let this go. But then I heard-- he-- when I was here in the United States, some friends came. They knew the story. They told me that he died. He did die of some heart problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: But because I-- maybe he never thought about that, or maybe he did. But after I reminded him in Australia, I'm sure he thought about it. And if he did, it didn't give him too many peaceful nights, if he remembered, because I reminded him what he did to me. Now, through the years here-- being here-- back in 1980, my youngest daughter, [? Shanyl, ?] Sylvia, got married. She became very religious. And they decided-- [? Shanyl ?] and her husband decided to move to Israel. Of course, to us, it was very heartbreaking in the beginning. So what it caused, seven trips to Israel. We went every couple years. Every year, every two years. It was hard. The telephone conversations, the letters, the yearning. We-- we-- it was really hard. And at the time she left, Dinah, our oldest daughter, was living in-- up north in Oakland. She went to school to a University of Hayward. But in '83, I think she came back-- moved back to LA. So at least we had her here. And the trips were nice. And back in '86, they adopted a little girl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this, I think, was in my life-- in Regina's life-- in Dinah's life-- and in all our lives-- was like a new sun came into our life. I mean, the sun up in the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the most beautiful thing, up until now, that happened to us. They adopted a beautiful little girl, and she is-- of course, we're spoiling here rotten. And these are the fights that we have with Sylvia. But what are grandparents for? And Dinah, also, loves here dearly. She's very close with Dinah. And what was it? Three years ago? 1991. The war-- I think the war-- the Gulf War made it. And [? Yohou, ?] my son-in-law, came here for a celebration for his father.

speaker: interviewee
text: His father-- a very prominent person here-- [? Nate ?] [? Barokas-- ?] achieved an award-- Life's Achievement Award in the [INAUDIBLE] Temple on Wilshire Boulevard. He was two years president there. And he achieved an award as the man of the year. And he brought his son out here. And this was in January, 1991, when the war in the Gulf was going to break out. And sure enough-- or the war was on already. And he came here. While he was here, we were on the phone with Sylvia continuously. And we heard the alarms over the telephone. And then I saw it on CNN here, when they were throwing the Scuds at them. And one Scud fell very close to them. She lived outside of [NON-ENGLISH]. And you can imagine how we all felt. This, together with things that didn't work out as well as they hoped they would, prompted them to decide to come back. Well, I don't want to go into what it did to us. This, this is self-explanatory. They stayed with us, I think, three months until they found an apartment. He is a handyman. He is a builder, electrician, carpentry. He works for himself now, and she has a job. And they're still very religious. And most of all that broke me up was the fact that I will not see my granddaughter grow up. All I had was a telephone. We saw her every year or so. I saw her when she was two years. When she was born-- they adopted her here in the United States. We saw her then. When she was 11 years old, they stayed here a couple of weeks. And then they came back-- after six months, they had to come back to-- for the-- to go to court where she was adopted, when it was finalized. And then I saw her when she was two years. And then they came back. And they came back. And we saw her in 1990 again. And in '91, they came back. Thank God. The family is complete. So, it's good. I hope she'll manage to come in here if it doesn't go in too close to Shabbat, because she's very religious. Like I said in the beginning, today is-- I was thinking about it the whole week, especially seeing everything on TV-- all the commemorations in Auschwitz, about the survivors of Auschwitz, which means-- includes all the people that survived, not just the-- because like, I didn't even know. They tell me the numbers-- that 5,200 people were liberated in Auschwitz. And I think they mean Auschwitz and Birkenau together, because I didn't see that many people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That day was always important to me, but not as important as today. This whole sense, we were-- we decided to have the interview today. And I wanted it on this day. How else can you celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't feel celebrated. I don't call it a celebration, even though I, I was left alive. Because like I said before, there are moments when I don't think it was such a big deal. But in another way, it's-- I shouldn't talk like that. I mean, it's, it's a crime to even talk like that. I have a family. I have children. I have a granddaughter. Of course, I'm glad I'm alive. And I'm glad that the memory still works-- that I remember all that. I'm glad that I'm surrounded with friends, good friends, that I have so much in common. No matter when we get together, no matter what the occasion is, at the end of the conversation, we're all back in the concentration camp or in the ghettos. And I think this is going to go on through the rest of our lives. I don't think I have any more to add to what I said already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Auschwitz? Was there another story that you had mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. In 1987, after about 10 or 15 years of arguing with myself, yes/no, yes/no, we finally went back to Poland. I already had tickets previously, and I cancelled them. I lost about $200 on the tickets. And I decided I didn't want to go. And then I finally-- and my doctor-- psychologist told me, Jack, for heaven's sake, go already, and close that book and forget it. Get it out of your system. Because you keep talking about it. It's going to nag the hell out of you until you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back. We went back for 12 days. I was in Warsaw. I was in Lodz, in my hometown. I was in the room where we lived in the apartment where I was born. I sat in there. They let us in.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I didn't want to go up. I was in the yard, looking up. And I took photos. And everyone-- the neighbors saw right away that an American is here. And we were dressed-- the camera and all, they thought it was police doing something, got scared. It was still under the Communists in 1987. And I said to our driver-- we had-- we, we lucked out. I had a taxi driver from Warsaw that I just picked him out. Just like that, he came over. He was supposed to take us something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I liked him. I liked the look of him. I said to him, [? Marek, ?] can you work with me for 12 days? Take me all over Poland? He said, yes, I'll do it. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: We agreed on a price, and he went with me. And he wasn't just my driver. He was my bodyguard. He watched over me every minute of the way. It's unbelievable what this guy-- we're still in contact with him seven years later. We just got letters with photos from the kids and everything. We send-- I send him money once in a while, as much as I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to them, you know what? Let's get out of here. I'm not going out. And [? Marek ?] said to me. Are you crazy? You mean you came all the way here, and you're not going to go up? You'll, you'll never forgive yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- and we went upstairs. And, of course, I recognized the banister with the, the sticks missing in between. The electric bulb with the-- with the wires-- with a rusty wire guard, just like before the war, because they were afraid people were going to steal the bulbs. And he knocked at the door. And the people there-- there were two ladies living there. And I went in there. And that was hard. That was very tough. I sat there looking at the ceiling. And I said, you know, something was changed here. I don't know what. The room is lower. I remember that ceiling, lying on my bed, looking up. It's not the same ceiling. They said, you are right. When we lived there, there were the beams sticking out and the crossbeams across. They modernized it. They covered the ceiling. That's why it's lower. They had no water-- no running water in the room. No toilet. But they had a little TV. And we were there maybe a half hour or so. I just sat there, took some photos, and looked around Lodz. Regina bought some postcards to send away. On one postcard there was a monument. It was called 'The Broken Heart' in Polish. A monument after the perished children of Lodz. Didn't say the Jewish children-- the perished children of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to [? Marek, ?] to our driver. I said, listen. I gotta see this. We drive around. We couldn't find-- finally, we found it. It was a huge monument in the shape of a heart-- maybe one-story high, cracked in the middle. And you see skeletons of two children walking through that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I usually didn't take photos with me or with Regina near the-- near these monuments-- just took [INAUDIBLE]. And it was surrounded with posts just like in Auschwitz, with the wires, and with names of all the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Regina said, you know what? I would like to have a photo here with live children-- with Polish children. And there were some kids playing-- it was a park, so all the-- so there were two children playing there. And since we spoke Polish, Regina asked the children, would you like to take a photo with me? So he said, I have to ask my mother, which we liked right away, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And about two minutes later, he came back. He said, my mother said it's OK. So I took a photo with the kids After that, Regina said, I've got to meet that mother of children now that speak like this. So we went down, and the mother was there with another, with a friend of a young man. And we got to talk. And then I found out it was very close to where we used to live in the ghetto. And I got to talking with this guy. And he said to me, you know, I have something at home that would interest you. My grandfather was a history buff, and so am I. And he left me something-- he died a long time ago-- that he found near the train station in Lodz on the garbage. He found it in 1946. He said, I think it's got Hebrew letters. I think it's from the Talmud. I heard it. I started to shake. I said, I'd love to see it. So he said, I think, to you, it would be very valuable. I said, let me just see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made arrangements to meet them in our hotel in the evening. I invited them to dinner. I also needed a map from Lodz. I couldn't get a map from Lodz anywhere. The hotel didn't have any maps. Not-- and I like when I walk somewhere-- I didn't remember Lodz that well. So he said, you know what? When I come to the hotel, I'll bring you a map-- in fact, I still have that map-- and I'll bring you that paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes to the hotel in the evening to dinner, and he lays out a piece of parchment all ripped off from the Torah-- from a Torah scroll-- only about that big. Oh-- I have the measurements. They gave it-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, you know what? To me, this has only a historical value. I think to you, it's a lot more important. I said, you bet it is. I said, tell me. How much do you want for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, it's not for sale. There is no price on it. I'll give it to you. Please, take it. I forced him practically-- I gave him either $5 or $10. Buy something for the children. And, of course, we became friends. And we still correspond. [? Pavel ?] [? Stolarek ?] is his name. He, he lives in Lodz. He was born in Lodz. And I brought this parchment home. So I said-- you know, when we were both talking-- and it was risky to take it out. You know, if the government found out, they would want this for the archives, for a museum. I put it in the suitcase with paper covered, and he was so scared. The driver said, my god, if they catch you with this, they'll arrest you. They didn't open the suitcases, and we brought it home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bring it home. I thought, what am I going to do with it? I wanted to frame it here, keep it here, but then I realized, all right. I'm going to look at it. Once the kids are going to see it. Maybe I'll frame it. I might ruin that thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I called up the [INAUDIBLE] Center-- the archive. They heard [INAUDIBLE] bring it over. Let's see if it's authentic. And they verified it, that it is authentic. It is from a, a real parchment-- not paper-- from the Torah. [SIGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they accepted it. I signed all the legal papers that I gave it away. And now in the new building, in the-- not in the old [INAUDIBLE] they have it in the hall where they have all the artifacts, you know? And it's there under a glass.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted from me-- he said-- I told him how I got it. They said, you know, the story how you got it is as important as the scroll itself. And I wrote to [? Pavel. ?] He wrote me a letter, how old his grandfather was. Regina suspects that the Grandfather was Jewish. I don't know who he worked for, who he was with. And I translated the whole letter in English. And part of the information they got printed now. It is under a plastic or whatever. And it's called 'The Lewin Scrolls.' This is my tombstone left for generations. I was up there once. They had some schools [INAUDIBLE]. I go there from time to time, because I'm a member. And I showed one of the docents. Oh, my god. They couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was glad I went to Poland, if for nothing else, for that. And I got it out of my system. And I don't think that I'll ever have the courage to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess that's all I can tell you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm all emptied out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you. And I know that your wife is here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you could bring her here so you can introduce her.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my wife, Regina Lewin-- [? Szwarcfeld-- ?] her maiden name. My wife of 48 years. And we hope very much to celebrate our 50th in a couple of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you explain a little bit about this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is me, Jack Lewin, taking-- taken-- I think this had to be around August-- no, later-- September, October 1945. I'm wearing here an American army uniform that I got when I was working in Furth by an-- at the airport that the Air Force kitchen in Furth. The name of the airport was R28 for Major Milton [? Pescin. ?] So we were-- we received this uniform, and I was very proud to take a photo in it. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling. Can you tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This was taken in Brussels. I even recognize the building behind it. [NON-ENGLISH]. The north train station. In fact, I'm still wearing-- the jacket I'm wearing is also from an American uniform. But it's dyed navy, because after a while, they didn't let us wear the uniform. We had to dye them. The other people on the photo-- [NON-ENGLISH] With him, we lived practically in the same building in the ghetto in Lodz, but we met afterwards. She's his girlfriend. Of course, now his wife-- also after 48 years of marriage at the moment. But that-- I think they were married then. With her, we walked through the border from Germany to Belgium. We smuggled together. He left with a transport before us. And I sort of took care of her for him. So that's them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one second. And can you tell us this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my family at a wedding-- a family wedding. On my right is my oldest daughter, Dinah. On my left is Regina. And on her left is our youngest daughter, Sylvia. The married daughter-- the religious one, as you can see with the hat on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what year was this picture taken, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think two years ago. I think this was taken-- was it '95? I think '93, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, if I remember correctly, at a Mother's Day outing for Regina. So in the photo is Regina, myself, and our gorgeous granddaughter, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.
